proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 16 binary features, at least one shows a univariate association with objective_response (chi-square p<0.05).
Among the 18 continuous features, at least one shows a univariate association with objective_response (t-test p<0.05).
feature_004 (ordinal, 3 levels — likely a performance status or risk score) is inversely associated with objective_response: higher feature_004 yields lower response rate.

analyses.code: from scipy import stats; [stats.chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], df['objective_response'])) for c in binary_cols+['feature_004']]
analyses.result_summary: Across binary/ordinal features: feature_004 (p=1.79e-90, response rates 23.1%, 17.4%, 12.9% across levels 0,1,2 — inverse), feature_014 (p=5.79e-70, rates 16.7% vs 23.5%), feature_001 (p=1.88e-55, 16.5% vs 22.1%), feature_021 (p=6.62e-51, 16.6% vs 22.0%), feature_026 (p=3.31e-06, 19.1% vs 16.8% — adverse), feature_009 (p=3.51e-05, 19.0% vs 16.6% — adverse). All other binaries (010, 005, 016, 025, 019, 015, 018, 008, 006, 032, 030) p>0.13.

analyses.code: from scipy import stats; [stats.ttest_ind(df.loc[df.objective_response==1, c], df.loc[df.objective_response==0, c]) for c in cont_cols]
analyses.result_summary: Strongest continuous associations: feature_020 (p=8.0e-31, mean 3.44 in responders vs 3.95 in non; diff=-0.51), feature_024 (p=2.2e-16, 58.5 vs 60.3), feature_029 (p=2.8e-07, 3.82 vs 3.79), feature_027 (p=6.0e-06, 5.69 vs 6.17), feature_022 (p=1.8e-04, 19.3 vs 20.4). feature_011 (age-like, range 18-92) is not associated with response (p=0.88).

proposed_hypotheses.text: After multivariable adjustment, the binary features feature_001, feature_014, feature_021 each retain independent positive associations with objective_response, suggesting they may represent distinct therapeutic agents.
After multivariable adjustment, feature_026 and feature_009 retain independent negative associations with objective_response, suggesting adverse biomarkers.
feature_018 and feature_008 are highly correlated (or possibly identical) given identical prevalence (15.09%).

analyses.code: import statsmodels.api as sm; X=sm.add_constant(df_std[all_features]); sm.Logit(df.objective_response, X).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Multivariable logistic regression on standardized features. Significant positive: feature_001 (coef +0.374, p<1e-50), feature_014 (+0.436, p<1e-50), feature_021 (+0.362, p<1e-50), feature_029 (+0.061, p=1.4e-07), feature_007 (+0.026, p=0.023). Significant negative: feature_004 (-0.362), feature_026 (-0.169), feature_009 (-0.188), feature_020 (-0.142), feature_024 (-0.100), feature_027 (-0.060), feature_022 (-0.048). Pseudo-R2 = 0.032 (modest — suggests important interactions are present).
Not identical: 12,704 rows differ. They share prevalence (15.09%) but are distinct binary indicators. Both appear roughly independent (joint=1193 vs expected ~1140 under independence).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_001, feature_014, and feature_021 have synergistic 2-way interactions on objective_response (i.e., the response rate when both are present exceeds the sum of their individual effects).

analyses.code: for a,b in itertools.combinations(binary_cols, 2): logistic with main effects + a*b interaction
analyses.result_summary: All three pairs show large positive interaction coefficients on the logit scale: feature_001 x feature_014: coef +1.060, p=6.8e-103; feature_014 x feature_021: +1.055, p=5.5e-102; feature_001 x feature_021: +0.883, p=1.1e-78. These dwarf all other pairwise binary interactions in the dataset (next largest unrelated pair: feature_010 x feature_032 coef +0.314, p=2.2e-04).

proposed_hypotheses.text: The three-way co-occurrence of feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1 is associated with a much higher objective_response rate than any subset (singletons or pairs), indicating a 3-way synergistic interaction.

analyses.code: df.groupby([f001, f014, f021]).objective_response.mean(); sm.Logit with f001, f014, f021, all 2-way and 3-way interaction terms
analyses.result_summary: Response rate by (feature_001, feature_014, feature_021) combination: 0_0_0=17.2% (n=12533), 0_0_1=16.2% (n=8383), 0_1_0=16.0% (n=5404), 0_1_1=15.7% (n=3624), 1_0_0=16.4% (n=8458), 1_0_1=16.9% (n=5612), 1_1_0=16.0% (n=3585), 1_1_1=63.3% (n=2401). All seven non-triple combinations cluster around the 16-17% baseline; only the triple-positive cell shows the large benefit. Saturated logistic model: 3-way interaction coefficient +2.126 (p=3.9e-92); main effects and 2-way interactions all near zero after accounting for the 3-way term.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the triple-positive subgroup (feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1), patients with feature_026=1 have a substantially lower response rate than those with feature_026=0 — i.e., feature_026 is a biomarker of treatment resistance specifically for this triple regimen.

Within the triple-positive subgroup, patients with feature_009=1 have a substantially lower response rate than those with feature_009=0 — feature_009 is also a marker of resistance to the triple regimen.

analyses.result_summary: Within the triple-positive group (n=2401): feature_026=0 → response 71.7% (n=2025); feature_026=1 → response 18.4% (n=376) — drop of 53 percentage points. feature_009=0 → response 69.1% (n=2125); feature_009=1 → response 18.8% (n=276) — drop of 50 percentage points. Both effects collapse the response back to the baseline rate of triple-negative patients. Other binary features within triple-positive group show no meaningful modulation (all |diff|<0.05).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Formal logistic-regression interaction terms triple x feature_026 and triple x feature_009 are significantly negative, while feature_026 and feature_009 have no main effect on response in the absence of the triple regimen.

analyses.code: X = const, triple, f026, f009, triple*f026, triple*f009; sm.Logit(y, X)
analyses.result_summary: Interaction model: triple coef +2.901 (p<1e-300); triple x feature_026 coef -2.660 (p=1.0e-69); triple x feature_009 coef -2.550 (p=8.6e-50). feature_026 main effect +0.019 (p=0.59), feature_009 main effect -0.005 (p=0.90) — neither has any effect on response in triple-negative patients. Within triple-negative patients, response is ~16.5% regardless of feature_026 or feature_009 status (16.5%, 16.5%, 16.8%, 15.8% across the four cells). This confirms that feature_026 and feature_009 are pure effect modifiers of the triple-regimen treatment effect, not prognostic markers.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Comprehensive interaction screen across all features will identify feature_026 and feature_009 as the only meaningful negative modifiers of the triple treatment effect.

analyses.code: for c in features: sm.Logit(y, [const, triple, c, triple*c]); record p of triple*c term
analyses.result_summary: Systematic screen of all 32 non-treatment features. Top interactions with triple: feature_026 (coef -2.439, p=1.8e-62), feature_009 (coef -2.259, p=4.3e-42). Next strongest: feature_024 (coef +0.152, p=5.6e-04) — much smaller magnitude. All other features have p > 0.1. feature_004 (the strong main-effect ordinal) shows NO modification of the treatment effect (p=0.34). This isolates feature_026 and feature_009 as the dominant treatment-effect modifiers.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The complete favorable subgroup is defined as: feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1 AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0. In this subgroup, objective_response rate exceeds 75%, compared with ~16% in all other patients.

analyses.result_summary: Ideal subgroup (n=1790, 3.6% of cohort): response = 78.6%. All other patients (n=48,210): response = 16.5%. Absolute risk difference = +62 percentage points. Chi-square p < 1e-300. Within the triple-positive group, the cleanest stratification is: triple-positive AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0 → 78.6% (n=1790); triple-positive with either feature_026=1 or feature_009=1 → 18.5% (n=611, weighted across the three off-cells). The two adverse markers act essentially as switches that turn off the treatment benefit.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The ideal-subgroup effect remains massive (OR > 10) after multivariable adjustment for all known main-effect prognostic features (feature_004, feature_011, feature_020, feature_022, feature_024, feature_027, feature_029).

analyses.code: X = const, ideal, feature_004, feature_011, feature_020, feature_022, feature_024, feature_027, feature_029; sm.Logit(y, X)
analyses.result_summary: Adjusted logistic model: ideal coef +3.002 (OR ~20.1; 95% CI 17.9-22.6; p<1e-300). Independent prognostic features retained: feature_004 (-0.392 per level), feature_020 (-0.156 per SD), feature_024 (-0.108 per SD), feature_022 (-0.056 per SD), feature_027 (-0.068 per SD), feature_029 (+0.065 per SD). feature_011 (age-like) p=0.83 — does not predict response. Pseudo-R2 = 0.082.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the ideal subgroup (n=1790), feature_004 (performance-status-like ordinal) further modulates response: the response rate decreases monotonically from feature_004=0 to feature_004=2.
Within the ideal subgroup, feature_020 (continuous, possibly peripheral blast count) is inversely associated with response, with non-responders having higher feature_020 values.

analyses.result_summary: Within the ideal subgroup, response rate by feature_004: level 0 = 86.0% (n=617), level 1 = 77.2% (n=902), level 2 = 66.8% (n=271). Within-ideal logistic: feature_004 coef -0.566 per level, p=1e-10. feature_004 modulates the magnitude of the ideal-subgroup benefit but does NOT abolish it (response remains high even at level 2). Consistent with feature_004 being a general prognostic factor (e.g., performance status) rather than a treatment-specific effect modifier.

analyses.code: from scipy import stats; stats.ttest_ind(ideal[ideal.objective_response==1].feature_020, ideal[ideal.objective_response==0].feature_020); within-ideal logistic with continuous covariates
analyses.result_summary: Within ideal subgroup: feature_020 mean is 3.77 in responders vs 4.56 in non-responders (t-test p=4.0e-04). Within-ideal multivariable logistic: feature_020 coef -0.206 per SD (p=2.8e-04). feature_029 also weakly positive (+0.119, p=0.045). Other continuous covariates (feature_011, 022, 024, 027) have p>0.08 within this subgroup.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Outside the triple-positive subgroup, no other 2- or 3-way binary feature combination yields a response rate substantially above the ~17% baseline; i.e., the triple regimen is the only effective treatment combination encoded in this dataset.

analyses.code: non = df[df.triple==0]; for a,b in itertools.combinations(binary_cols, 2): for av,bv: rate in non[(non[a]==av)&(non[b]==bv)]
analyses.result_summary: Among 47,599 triple-negative patients, top 10 binary 2-way subgroups (response rates): feature_019=1+feature_009=1: 19.8% (n=348); feature_018=1+feature_006=1: 18.7% (n=534); feature_016=1+feature_015=1: 18.4% (n=874); all other combinations within ±2 percentage points of the 16.5% baseline. Brute-force 3-way binary screen yields no triple-negative subgroup above 22% response. Confirms the triple regimen (feature_001+014+021) is the only therapeutic signal in this dataset.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Comprehensive 3-way interaction screen across all 16 binary features will rank feature_001 x feature_014 x feature_021 as by far the strongest positive 3-way interaction in the dataset.

analyses.code: for a,b,c in combinations(binary_cols,3): saturated logistic with all 2-way and 3-way interaction terms
analyses.result_summary: Top 3-way interaction (positive): feature_001 x feature_014 x feature_021 coef +2.126, p=3.9e-92. All other top 3-way interactions are NEGATIVE and explained by the action of feature_026 or feature_009 against one of the three treatments: feature_026 x feature_014 x feature_021 (-1.21, p=3e-16), feature_001 x feature_026 x feature_014 (-1.14, p=2e-14), feature_001 x feature_026 x feature_021 (-0.86, p=3e-10), and similar terms for feature_009. No other positive 3-way subgroup is identified. This rules out the existence of a hidden alternative effective treatment combination.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Treatment-effect heterogeneity for the triple regimen (feature_001 x feature_014 x feature_021) is driven primarily by two binary modifiers (feature_026 and feature_009) rather than by continuous lab values or demographic features.
Among continuous features, feature_024 has a weakly positive interaction with the triple regimen (i.e., higher feature_024 may be associated with a slightly larger absolute treatment benefit).

analyses.code: interaction screen ranks: triple x feature_026 (p=2e-62), triple x feature_009 (p=4e-42), triple x feature_024 (p=6e-04), all others p>0.11
analyses.result_summary: After Bonferroni correction across 32 features (alpha=0.05/32=1.6e-03), only feature_026, feature_009, and feature_024 survive as treatment-effect modifiers. The first two are dominant (effect sizes -2.44 and -2.26 on the logit scale); feature_024 is weak (+0.15) and may be a chance finding given the multiple testing burden. Confirms that the cleanest subgroup definition uses the two binary modifiers.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Final treatment-effect-heterogeneity hypothesis: among patients receiving the combination feature_001=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_021=1, those WITHOUT feature_026 AND WITHOUT feature_009 achieve an objective_response rate of ~79%, compared with ~17% in patients not receiving the full combination AND in patients receiving the combination but having either feature_026=1 or feature_009=1.

analyses.result_summary: FINAL SUBGROUP DEFINITION: ideal=1 (n=1790, 3.58% of cohort) → 78.6% response; ideal=0 (n=48,210, 96.42% of cohort) → 16.5% response. Risk difference = +62.1 percentage points (95% CI ~+60 to +64); risk ratio = 4.78. Within-strata sanity check: triple-positive AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=0 = 78.6% (n=1790); triple-positive AND feature_026=1 AND feature_009=0 = 18.2% (n=335); triple-positive AND feature_026=0 AND feature_009=1 = 18.7% (n=235); triple-positive AND both adverse = 19.5% (n=41). Among triple-negative patients, response is ~16-17% in every (feature_026, feature_009) cell. Effect persists in adjusted logistic regression (OR ~20).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Final summary hypothesis: in this AML cohort, the only large-magnitude treatment-effect signal is the combination of three binary indicators (feature_001 + feature_014 + feature_021) that together produce a synergistic ~78.6% objective response, but this synergistic benefit is completely abolished by the presence of either of two adverse biomarkers (feature_026 OR feature_009). General prognostic factors (feature_004, feature_020, feature_022, feature_024, feature_027, feature_029) modulate response magnitude but do not abolish the treatment effect.

analyses.code: Comprehensive synthesis of all preceding analyses
analyses.result_summary: The dataset displays one dominant treatment-effect heterogeneity pattern: the conjunction of three putative therapeutic indicators (feature_001, feature_014, feature_021) is required for response and is necessary for any large benefit. Two binary adverse biomarkers (feature_026 and feature_009) act as switches that turn the treatment effect off; their presence, in the context of the triple regimen, is associated with response rates indistinguishable from the no-treatment baseline. No alternative effective treatment combinations were identified by exhaustive 2-way or 3-way binary subgroup search. General prognostic features modify response magnitude but not the existence of the treatment effect. This pattern is statistically very strong (interaction p<1e-90) and clinically interpretable as a treatment-by-biomarker interaction with two independent resistance markers.
